events.title: Homeland Security Act Signed
events.detail: President Bush signs the Homeland Security Act of 2002, authorizing the creation of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS). The law merges 22 federal agencies, including the legacy Immigration and Naturalization Service (INS) and U.S. Customs Service, setting the stage for ICE's formation.

events.title: ICE Officially Established
events.detail: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) is officially created within DHS, merging the investigative and interior enforcement functions of the INS and U.S. Customs Service. ICE inherits approximately 20,000 employees and a broad mandate covering immigration enforcement, customs violations, and national security investigations.

events.title: Operation Predator Launched
events.detail: ICE launches Operation Predator, a nationwide enforcement program targeting individuals who prey on children, including sex trafficking, child pornography, and exploitation. Over the following years, the operation would result in thousands of arrests annually, becoming one of ICE's most publicized enforcement programs.

events.title: 287(g) Program Expanded
events.detail: ICE begins aggressively signing Memoranda of Agreement (MOAs) with local law enforcement agencies under Section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. These partnerships deputize local officers to perform immigration enforcement functions, significantly expanding ICE's reach beyond its own agents.

sources.name: ACLU — 287(g) Program: Flawed and Fatally So

events.title: Operation Return to Sender
events.detail: ICE launches Operation Return to Sender, a nationwide fugitive operations program targeting immigration absconders who had received final orders of removal but remained in the country. The operation would continue for years, resulting in tens of thousands of arrests annually. Critics noted that many arrested were not the high-priority targets originally described.

events.title: Swift & Company Meatpacking Raids (Operation Wagon Train)
events.detail: ICE executes simultaneous raids on six Swift & Company meatpacking plants in six states (Colorado, Iowa, Minnesota, Nebraska, Texas, Utah), arresting 1,297 workers suspected of identity theft and immigration violations. At the time, it was the largest worksite enforcement action in U.S. history. Hundreds of U.S. citizens who had their identities stolen were also identified.

sources.name: AP — ICE Raids Swift Plants Nationwide

events.title: Removals Surpass 319,000
events.detail: ICE reports 319,382 formal removals for Fiscal Year 2007, up from 280,974 in FY2006. The consistent year-over-year rise reflects the Bush administration's push to scale up enforcement capacity. Interior removals increasingly target immigrants without criminal records, drawing criticism from advocacy groups.

sources.name: DHS OHSS Yearbook of Immigration Statistics, Table 39

events.title: Secure Communities Program Piloted
events.detail: ICE pilots the Secure Communities program, which automatically shares fingerprints of individuals booked into local jails with the DHS IDENT database. The pilot begins in 14 jurisdictions. The program allows ICE to place detainer requests on individuals with immigration violations who are arrested for any offense, including minor infractions.

events.title: Postville, Iowa: Largest Single-Site Worksite Raid in U.S. History
events.detail: ICE arrests 389 workers at the Agriprocessors Inc. kosher meatpacking plant in Postville, Iowa — the largest single-site worksite enforcement action in U.S. history. Workers were charged with federal identity fraud crimes; most were from Guatemala and Mexico. The raid devastated the local community; Postville's population fell dramatically and the plant eventually filed for bankruptcy.

sources.name: AP — 389 Arrested in Iowa Plant Raid
NYT — Immigration Raid Leaves Iowa Town Reeling

events.title: Annual Removals Surpass 240,000
events.detail: ICE records 240,665 formal removals in Fiscal Year 2004, continuing an upward trend from the ICE baseline of 211,098 in FY2003. The Bush administration accelerates enforcement through expanded worksite operations, fugitive ops teams, and increased cooperation with local law enforcement under 287(g).

sources.name: DHS OHSS Yearbook of Immigration Statistics, Table 39

events.title: Operation Janus: 206,000 Fraudulent Naturalization Cases Identified
events.detail: ICE launches Operation Janus, an audit of INS-era naturalization records, identifying approximately 206,000 cases where fingerprints are missing — raising potential fraud concerns. The operation eventually refers approximately 950 denaturalization cases for prosecution over the following years. Critics argue it ensnares many innocent naturalized citizens.

sources.name: DOJ / ICE — Operation Janus Press Release

events.title: E-Verify Program Expanded
events.detail: DHS expands the E-Verify electronic employment eligibility verification system, which allows employers to check employees' immigration status against DHS and Social Security Administration databases. Federal contractors are required to use E-Verify. The program becomes central to the ICE strategy of reducing employment incentives for undocumented immigration.

events.title: Obama Administration: Shift to 'Convicted Criminal' Priorities
events.detail: Obama takes office promising comprehensive immigration reform while initially maintaining enforcement intensity. DHS Secretary Janet Napolitano signals a shift toward prioritizing convicted criminals and national security threats for deportation, though removals would continue to rise. Critics note the prioritization framework is slow to be implemented on the ground.

events.title: Secure Communities Officially Launched Nationwide
events.detail: DHS officially launches Secure Communities for national rollout. The program creates an automatic fingerprint-sharing pipeline between local jails and DHS. Advocates protest that the program leads to deportation of non-criminals and deters crime reporting in immigrant communities. By 2012, the program covers nearly all jurisdictions in the U.S.

events.title: Removals Surpass 390,000
events.detail: ICE reports 390,413 formal removals for Fiscal Year 2011. The Obama administration is deporting immigrants at a higher rate than any previous administration, earning him the nickname 'Deporter in Chief' from some immigration advocates. Two-thirds of those deported have criminal convictions, ICE contends, though critics dispute how 'criminal' is defined.

events.title: Morton Memos: Prosecutorial Discretion Guidance
events.detail: ICE Director John Morton issues a series of memos outlining prosecutorial discretion guidance, directing agents to de-prioritize enforcement against low-risk individuals such as veterans, pregnant women, students, and people with long community ties. Advocates welcome the policy but note implementation is inconsistent at the field level.

events.title: DACA Announced — 800,000 Protected from Removal
events.detail: President Obama announces the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA) program through executive action. DACA grants two-year renewable reprieves from deportation and work authorization to approximately 800,000 undocumented individuals brought to the U.S. as children. ICE is directed not to initiate enforcement proceedings against DACA recipients.

events.title: All-Time Record: 432,201 Removals
events.detail: ICE records 432,201 formal removals in Fiscal Year 2013 — the highest single-year total in the agency's history. The milestone sparks intense criticism from immigration advocates who label Obama the 'Deporter in Chief.' The figure includes both criminal and non-criminal removals and border apprehensions reframed as removals.

events.title: Flores Settlement Reaffirmed: 20-Day Limit on Child Detention
events.detail: U.S. District Judge Dolly Gee reaffirms and extends the 1997 Flores Settlement Agreement, ruling that the government must release children from detention within 20 days, house them in non-secure, licensed facilities, and use the least restrictive setting available. The ruling directly challenges the Obama administration's family detention expansion at Dilley and Karnes City, Texas.

sources.name: Flores v. Reno — U.S. District Court C.D. Cal., Aug 2015

events.title: Obama Ends Secure Communities, Announces DAPA
events.detail: President Obama announces executive actions ending the Secure Communities program and replacing it with the Priority Enforcement Program (PEP), which is intended to focus only on serious criminals. He also announces Deferred Action for Parents of Americans (DAPA), potentially shielding up to 4 million parents of U.S. citizens from deportation. Texas and 25 other states immediately sue to block DAPA.

events.title: Federal Judge Hanen Blocks DAPA
events.detail: U.S. District Judge Andrew Hanen in Brownsville, Texas issues a preliminary injunction blocking implementation of DAPA and DACA expansion. Hanen finds that the Obama administration violated the Administrative Procedure Act by issuing major policy without notice-and-comment rulemaking. The Fifth Circuit upholds the injunction and the Supreme Court deadlocks 4-4, effectively killing DAPA.

sources.name: Texas v. United States, S.D. Tex., Feb 16, 2015
NYT — Judge Blocks Obama Immigration Orders

events.title: Senate Passes Gang of 8 Immigration Bill — House Blocks It
events.detail: The U.S. Senate passes S.744, the Border Security, Economic Opportunity, and Immigration Modernization Act, with bipartisan 68-32 vote. The bill would have created a path to citizenship for approximately 11 million undocumented immigrants while significantly increasing border and interior enforcement. House Speaker Boehner refuses to bring it to the floor, ending the last major comprehensive reform effort.

events.title: Detainee Hunger Strikes Spread Across ICE Facilities
events.detail: Coordinated hunger strikes by immigration detainees spread across multiple ICE facilities in April 2013, protesting detention conditions, length of detention, lack of access to lawyers, and deportation policies. Strikes occur at Tacoma, Washington; Adelanto, California; and other facilities. ICE responds with force-feeding orders at some facilities, drawing condemnation from medical and human rights organizations.

events.title: Obama Expands Family Detention in Response to Border Surge
events.detail: Facing a surge of Central American families and unaccompanied children at the border in summer 2014, the Obama administration dramatically expands family detention capacity. The Karnes County Residential Center opens and the Dilley South Texas Family Residential Center is contracted. Advocates criticize family detention expansion as contrary to Obama's stated immigration values.

era: Trump First Term: Zero Tolerance & Family Separation (2017–2021)

events.title: EO 13768: Enhanced Interior Enforcement
events.detail: President Trump signs Executive Order 13768 'Enhancing Public Safety in the Interior of the United States,' expanding ICE enforcement priorities to essentially all undocumented immigrants rather than the limited criminal categories of the Obama era. The order also directs expansion of 287(g) partnerships and resumption of Secure Communities.

events.title: Secure Communities Formally Reinstated
events.detail: DHS Secretary John Kelly issues implementing guidance reinstating the Secure Communities program that Obama had ended in 2014. The reinstatement effectively expands the fingerprint-sharing pipeline nationwide again, enabling ICE to lodge detainer requests on any individual booked into a local jail with any immigration violation.

events.title: Interior ICE Arrests Surge 43%
events.detail: ICE reports a 43% increase in interior immigration arrests in the first weeks of the Trump administration compared to the same period in 2016. Non-criminal arrests — those with no prior criminal conviction — increase by the most dramatic percentage, signaling a fundamental shift from Obama-era priorities. Fear of enforcement spreads widely in immigrant communities.

sources.name: Pew Research Center — Arrests Surge Under Trump

events.title: Operation Safe City: Targeting Sanctuary Jurisdictions
events.detail: ICE conducts Operation Safe City, a four-day enforcement surge targeting 10 jurisdictions with sanctuary policies including New York, Los Angeles, Chicago, Denver, and Philadelphia. ICE arrests 498 individuals and declares the operation a warning to jurisdictions that refuse to cooperate with ICE detainer requests.

events.title: AG Sessions Announces Zero Tolerance Policy
events.detail: Attorney General Jeff Sessions announces the 'zero tolerance' policy directing federal prosecutors to criminally prosecute every adult crossing the border illegally, including asylum seekers presenting at ports of entry. Because parents are transferred to federal criminal custody for prosecution, children cannot remain with them and are separated into HHS custody — triggering the family separation crisis.

sources.name: AP — AG Sessions Zero Tolerance Order

events.title: Family Separation Crisis: 2,700+ Children Separated
events.detail: Within six weeks of zero tolerance implementation, over 2,700 children are separated from parents at the U.S.-Mexico border. Children as young as infants are placed in HHS Office of Refugee Resettlement shelters. ProPublica publishes audio of children crying for their parents, triggering massive public outcry. DHS Inspector General later finds the government never developed a plan to reunify families.

events.title: Federal Judge Orders Family Reunification in Ms. L v. ICE
events.detail: U.S. District Judge Dana Sabraw in San Diego issues a landmark class-action order in Ms. L v. ICE, requiring the Trump administration to reunite all separated children with their parents within 30 days (14 days for children under 5). The court finds that the government's conduct shocks the conscience and may have violated due process. HHS and ICE fail to meet the initial deadlines.

sources.name: Ms. L v. ICE, S.D. Cal., Jun 26, 2018

events.title: Trump Signs EO Ending Family Separation
events.detail: Facing bipartisan condemnation and international pressure, President Trump signs an executive order directing that families be detained together at the border rather than separated. However, because the Flores settlement limits family detention to 20 days, the order creates a new conflict between the administration's desire to detain families indefinitely and existing court rulings.

sources.name: White House — EO on Family Detention, Jun 20, 2018
NYT — Trump Signs Order to End Family Separation

events.title: DHS Attempts to Terminate Flores Agreement
events.detail: DHS issues a final rule attempting to replace the Flores Settlement Agreement with new regulations that would allow indefinite family detention. U.S. District Judge Dolly Gee issues a preliminary injunction blocking the rule, finding it is 'not Flores compliant.' The administration's effort to eliminate the 20-day limit on child detention continues through multiple court rounds.

sources.name: Flores v. Barr, C.D. Cal., Sep 2019

events.title: 21 Deaths in ICE Custody — Highest Recorded
events.detail: ICE reports 21 deaths in custody during Fiscal Year 2020 — the highest annual total in the agency's recorded history at that time. The spike coincides with the COVID-19 pandemic; multiple detention facilities experience outbreaks with inadequate infection control. Advocacy groups file emergency litigation seeking releases; most fail. COVID-19 deaths account for a significant share of the FY2020 total.

events.title: Title 42 Invoked: Mass Expulsions Begin
events.detail: The Trump CDC invokes Title 42 of the U.S. Code to authorize the mass expulsion of migrants at the border without processing asylum claims, citing COVID-19 public health concerns. Unlike formal ICE removals, Title 42 expulsions do not count in official removal statistics. Hundreds of thousands are expelled under Title 42 over the next three years.

events.title: SCOTUS: Expedited Removal Detainees Lack Habeas Rights
events.detail: The Supreme Court rules 7-2 in Department of Homeland Security v. Thuraissigiam that individuals in expedited removal proceedings do not have a constitutional right to use federal habeas corpus to seek a full asylum hearing. The ruling dramatically limits judicial oversight of ICE's expedited removal authority, affecting hundreds of thousands of cases annually.

events.title: DHS Secretary Nielsen Resigns Under Trump Pressure
events.detail: DHS Secretary Kirstjen Nielsen resigns after President Trump reportedly demands she resume family separation, engage in illegal conduct, and further restrict asylum processing. Nielsen had implemented zero tolerance but resisted some extreme measures. Her departure signals an even harder enforcement line in the final two years of Trump's first term.

events.title: Migrant Protection Protocols (Remain in Mexico) Implemented
events.detail: DHS implements the Migrant Protection Protocols (MPP), also known as 'Remain in Mexico,' requiring asylum seekers who present at the southern border to wait in Mexico while their U.S. immigration cases are adjudicated. Over 70,000 migrants were enrolled before Biden ended the program in 2021. Advocates document dangerous conditions in Mexican border cities where enrollees awaited hearings.

events.title: ICE Detention Population Reaches 55,000+ — Pre-2025 Record
events.detail: ICE detention population exceeds 55,000 individuals in November 2019 — a record at the time and double the Obama-era detention numbers. The surge is driven by Trump's universal enforcement priorities and border enforcement surge. ICE scrambles to find bed space by contracting with additional county jails and private facilities.

events.title: SCOTUS: No Automatic Bond Hearings for Long-Term Detainees
events.detail: The Supreme Court rules 5-3 in Jennings v. Rodriguez that immigration statutes do not automatically require periodic bond hearings for individuals in mandatory or prolonged detention. Justice Alito writes for the majority that the statutory language is clear. The ruling is a major blow to detainees held for months or years without a bail hearing; the constitutional question is remanded to the Ninth Circuit.

events.title: Biden DHS Issues New Enforcement Priorities
events.detail: DHS Secretary Alejandro Mayorkas issues guidance narrowing ICE enforcement priorities to three categories: national security threats, recent border crossers, and individuals convicted of aggravated felonies. Interior enforcement arrests drop dramatically. Critics argue the guidance effectively creates a de facto non-enforcement policy for most undocumented immigrants.

events.title: Removals Plummet to 89,191 Under Biden
events.detail: ICE reports just 89,191 formal removals for Fiscal Year 2021 — a drop of approximately 62% from FY2019 levels and the lowest total since the early 2000s. The combination of Biden enforcement priorities, COVID-19, and a shift toward Title 42 expulsions accounts for the decline. Interior removals fall most sharply, with ICE de-emphasizing non-criminal cases.

events.title: Texas and Louisiana Sue Over Biden Enforcement Priorities
events.detail: Texas and Louisiana file suit challenging the Biden administration's narrowed ICE enforcement priorities, arguing the federal government has an obligation to enforce immigration law and cannot simply choose to ignore removals. Judge Drew Tipton in Texas issues multiple injunctions blocking the priorities. The litigation eventually reaches the Supreme Court.

sources.name: WaPo — States Sue Over ICE Priorities

events.title: SCOTUS Upholds Biden ICE Enforcement Discretion
events.detail: The Supreme Court rules 8-1 in United States v. Texas that the states of Texas and Louisiana lack standing to challenge the Biden administration's ICE enforcement priorities. The ruling allows the executive branch to exercise prosecutorial discretion in immigration enforcement. Justice Kavanaugh writes the majority opinion; only Justice Alito dissents.

events.title: Historic Ms. L v. ICE Settlement Approved
events.detail: U.S. District Judge Dana Sabraw approves a landmark settlement in Ms. L v. ICE, requiring ongoing remediation for families separated under the Trump zero-tolerance policy. The settlement establishes a process for separated families to apply for compensation and immigration relief. At least 4,600 children were separated; hundreds of families have not been reunified years later.

sources.name: Ms. L v. ICE — Settlement Order, Dec 8, 2023

events.title: FY2024: 113,431 Administrative Arrests
events.detail: ICE reports 113,431 administrative arrests for Fiscal Year 2024, a moderate increase from FY2023 as the Biden administration responds to political pressure on border security. Deaths in ICE custody reach 12 for the fiscal year. The detention population remains elevated compared to the Biden administration's early tenure.

events.title: ICE Expands Alternatives to Detention (ATD) Program
events.detail: Under the Biden administration, ICE dramatically expands the Alternatives to Detention (ATD) program using GPS ankle monitoring, SmartLINK app check-ins, and case management services. ATD enrollment grows to over 200,000 participants — a record. The program achieves over 90% compliance with immigration court hearings at a fraction of the cost of physical detention. The expansion is hailed by advocates as a model for humane enforcement.

events.title: Biden Ends Migrant Protection Protocols (Remain in Mexico)
events.detail: President Biden orders the termination of the Migrant Protection Protocols on his second day in office. DHS Secretary Mayorkas issues guidance ending new MPP enrollments. However, Texas and other states sue and federal courts order the administration to restart MPP in 2021 and 2022. The Supreme Court ultimately rules in Biden v. Texas that the administration has discretion to end MPP.

sources.name: White House — EO on MPP, Jan 20, 2021

events.title: Title 42 Ends After COVID-19 Public Health Emergency Expires
events.detail: The Biden administration ends Title 42 expulsions as the federal COVID-19 public health emergency expires. Over 2.8 million expulsions occurred under Title 42 between March 2020 and May 2023. The administration implements a new suite of border enforcement measures including a new asylum processing rule restricting claims. A brief surge in border crossings follows the end of Title 42.

events.title: Zadvydas v. Davis: Indefinite Post-Removal Detention Unconstitutional
events.detail: The Supreme Court rules 5-4 in Zadvydas v. Davis that once a person has been ordered removed but actual removal is not reasonably foreseeable (such as when no country will accept them), continued detention must have a reasonable time limit — presumptively six months. The ruling shapes ICE detention authority for the next two decades and applies to individuals from countries that refuse to accept deportees.

era: Trump Second Term: Mass Deportation Campaign (2025–Present)

events.title: Trump Signs EO 'Protecting the American People Against Invasion'
events.detail: On his first day of the second term, President Trump signs Executive Order 'Protecting the American People Against Invasion,' removing all categorical enforcement priorities established under Biden. All undocumented immigrants in the United States are now subject to arrest and removal. ICE is directed to arrest as many people as possible, triggering an unprecedented enforcement surge.

sources.name: White House — EO 'Protecting the American People,' Jan 20, 2025

events.title: ICE Detention Population Hits Record 70,766
events.detail: ICE detention population reaches 70,766 individuals in February 2025 — a record high. The rapid expansion strains existing facilities and ICE scrambles to contract with additional county jails and private detention facilities. TRAC analysis finds 73.6% of detainees have no criminal convictions; the administration continues to frame detentions as targeting dangerous criminals.

sources.name: Detention Watch Network — State of Detention 2025

events.title: Alien Enemies Act of 1798 Invoked to Deport Gang Members
events.detail: The Trump administration invokes the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 — a wartime statute — to deport alleged members of the Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua to El Salvador's maximum security CECOT prison. The president declares Venezuela is conducting an 'invasion' of the United States. Advocates note the statute has only been used in wartime against citizens of enemy nations.

sources.name: White House — Alien Enemies Act Proclamation, Mar 2025

events.title: SCOTUS Rules 5-4 for Trump on Alien Enemies Act Deportations
events.detail: The Supreme Court rules 5-4 in Trump v. J.G.G. that lower courts exceeded their authority in issuing a temporary restraining order blocking Alien Enemies Act deportations. The per curiam opinion allows deportation flights to El Salvador to resume, though the court does not rule on the merits of the underlying Alien Enemies Act claim. The ruling is widely seen as a major victory for the administration's deportation agenda.

events.title: 24 Deaths in ICE Custody — New Annual Record
events.detail: ICE reports 24 deaths in custody during Fiscal Year 2025, with the Detention Watch Network counting 31 for the calendar year — both all-time records. Advocates attribute the increase to overcrowding, inadequate medical staffing, and rapid facility expansion with insufficient vetting of standards. ICE publishes required 90-day reports for each death.

events.title: 39,694 ICE Detention Bookings in January Alone
events.detail: TRAC reports 39,694 individuals booked into ICE detention in January 2026, compared to monthly averages of approximately 12,000 under the Biden administration. The surge reflects the full implementation of the Trump second-term enforcement agenda with no categorical exemptions. Interior enforcement operations in major cities produce nightly news footage of mass arrests.

events.title: Minnesota Class Action Filed Over Mass Arrests Without Warrants
events.detail: A class-action lawsuit, Hussen v. Noem, is filed in the District of Minnesota seeking an injunction against ICE's use of suspicionless stops, warrantless arrests, and alleged racial profiling in the enforcement surge. The case challenges whether the Fourth Amendment's protections against unreasonable searches and seizures apply to ICE's mass arrest operations.

sources.name: Hussen v. Noem, D. Minn., filed Jan 15, 2026

events.title: E-Verify Mandated for All U.S. Employers — Trump EO
events.detail: Trump's Day One executive actions include a mandate expanding E-Verify requirements to all U.S. employers, not just federal contractors. The move is intended to cut off undocumented immigrants' access to employment. Implementation timelines and enforcement mechanisms are gradually established through subsequent DHS and DOL rulemaking.

sources.name: White House — EO on E-Verify, Jan 2025

events.title: ICE Wrongfully Arrests U.S. Citizens in Mass Enforcement Surge
events.detail: Multiple credible reports emerge of U.S. citizens being arrested by ICE during the 2025 enforcement surge. Cases include native-born citizens and lawful permanent residents detained based on apparent race or ethnicity. The ACLU and immigration lawyers file emergency habeas petitions; some individuals are held for days before documentation is verified. Civil rights organizations document dozens of such incidents.

events.title: 29 Deaths in ICE Custody Break 22-Year Record in FY2026
events.detail: ICE custody deaths in Fiscal Year 2026 (October 2025–present) reached 29 by April 18, 2026, breaking the 22-year record of 28 set in FY2004. The calendar year 2026 total stands at 17 — approximately one death per week. Advocacy groups and medical experts attribute the record toll to overcrowding at rapidly expanded facilities, ICE's October 3, 2025 decision to stop paying for third-party medical care, and inadequate staffing at converted warehouses and military facilities used for detention. Only 15 of 49 deaths since January 2025 received required 48-hour interim notices to Congress. A Fort Bliss, Texas military detention facility recorded a death ruled homicide. Democracy Now! reported on April 21 that Setareh Ghandehari of Detention Watch Network described the toll as unlike anything previously documented.

events.title: Acting ICE Director Todd Lyons Announces Resignation Effective May 31, 2026
events.detail: Todd Lyons, Sr. Official Performing the Duties of ICE Director since March 2025, announced on April 16-17, 2026 that he will resign effective May 31, 2026. Lyons cited a desire to return home before his younger son finishes high school and to pursue a private sector opportunity. He led ICE through one of its most aggressive enforcement eras, overseeing the detention population peak at 70,000+, Operation Metro Surge in Minneapolis, record deportation numbers, and the record pace of in-custody deaths. DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin confirmed the departure; no successor has been named as of April 22. Lyons said he has been asked to help identify candidates for the permanent director position, which requires Senate confirmation.

events.title: 11th Circuit Allows 'Alligator Alcatraz' Everglades Detention Facility to Remain Open
events.detail: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the 11th Circuit vacated a preliminary injunction that had blocked ICE's 'Alligator Alcatraz' detention facility near the Florida Everglades, ruling the district court 'abused its discretion.' The facility — a converted warehouse with capacity for 1,500 detainees in an environmentally sensitive area — was challenged by the Friends of the Everglades, Center for Biological Diversity, and Miccosukee Tribe for bypassing required environmental review. The 11th Circuit's ruling allows the facility to operate while litigation continues, representing a significant legal win for the administration's detention expansion agenda.
